aliases.name: Old Hag
aliases.meaning: Traditional name for sleep paralysis entity

description.summary: Supernatural entity believed to cause sleep paralysis across cultures. Described as an old hag or malevolent presence that sits on the sleeper's chest, preventing movement and speech during sleep.
description.full: The Night Hag is a supernatural entity reported across nearly every human culture, believed to cause the phenomenon of sleep paralysis. Victims awaken but find themselves unable to move or speak, often experiencing a crushing pressure on the chest, difficulty breathing, and an overwhelming sense of dread or malevolent presence. Modern science identifies this as sleep paralysis—a well-documented medical condition occurring during transitions between sleep stages where REM atonia persists into waking consciousness. Despite the scientific explanation, the remarkable consistency of cultural interpretations worldwide makes the Night Hag a fascinating intersection of physiology, psychology, and folklore.

etymology.origin: The term 'Night Hag' refers to the traditional image of an old witch or crone that visits sleepers at night.
etymology.meaning: A malevolent female spirit that oppresses sleepers
etymology.cultural_variations: Nearly universal across cultures with local variations

related_phenomena.name: Succubus/Incubus
related_phenomena.description: Sexual demons associated with sleep paralysis

appearance.description: Often described as an old woman or hag, though appearance varies
Feels extremely heavy when sitting on chest
Sense of malevolent entity in room, often unseen
May appear as dark shadow or silhouette

physical.morphology: Manifestation varies by culture but consistently associated with oppressive weight and malevolent presence

abilities.description: Creates sensation of crushing weight on chest
Victim cannot speak or call for help

behavior.attack_pattern: Visits during sleep transitions, particularly during REM sleep

range.description: Universal human experience reported across all cultures and geographic locations
range.cultural_universality: Nearly every culture has some version of the Night Hag entity

cultural_variations.name: Old Hag
cultural_variations.description: Traditional term for sleep paralysis experience

origins.cultural_roots: Sleep paralysis has been documented throughout human history across all cultures. Universal human physiology creates consistent experience interpreted through cultural lens.
origins.earliest_accounts: Ancient civilizations including Mesopotamia, Egypt, Greece, and Rome documented similar experiences
origins.folklore_connections: Connected to worldwide witch, demon, and spirit traditions

timeline.significance: Sleep paralysis recognized and attributed to supernatural entities
Early medical documentation of sleep paralysis symptoms
Formal medical recognition of sleep paralysis
timeline.source: Transactions of the College of Physicians of Philadelphia

timeline.significance: Scientific explanation of mechanism behind sleep paralysis
Full physiological understanding of sleep paralysis phenomenon

history.modern_era: Sleep paralysis is now well-understood medically as a parasomnia occurring during transitions between sleep stages. Despite scientific explanation, cultural interpretations persist.

typical_episode.onset: Usually occurs during sleep-wake transition, particularly when falling asleep or waking up
typical_episode.duration: Typically lasts seconds to minutes, though may feel much longer
stages: Awareness of waking but inability to move
Sense of presence or entity in room

physiology.mechanism: During REM sleep, the body experiences atonia (muscle paralysis) to prevent acting out dreams. Sleep paralysis occurs when consciousness returns before atonia ends, leaving the person awake but unable to move.
physiology.brain_regions: Involves interactions between brainstem (controlling atonia), amygdala (processing fear), and cortical areas (consciousness)

scientific_explanation.hypnagogic_hallucinations: Vivid sensory experiences during sleep-wake transitions, often threatening in nature due to amygdala activation
scientific_explanation.status: Fully explained medical phenomenon with well-understood physiological mechanisms

folklore.beliefs: Traditionally interpreted as attack by witch, demon, ghost, or malevolent spirit

literature: Various horror literature featuring sleep paralysis
film_tv: Horror films featuring sleep paralysis scenes
art: Contemporary sleep paralysis art and photography

sources.title: Sleep Paralysis: Historical, Psychological, and Medical Perspectives
The Terror That Comes in the Night: An Experience-Centered Study of Supernatural Assault Traditions
Sleep Paralysis: Night-mares, Nocebos, and the Mind-Body Connection
